Math401Math 401, Summer 2025: Freiwald research project notesMath 401, Topic 4: The quantum version of probabilistic concepts

Math401 Topic 4: The quantum version of probabilistic concepts

In mathematics, on often speaks of non-commutative instead of quantum constructions.

Note, in this section, we will see a lot of mixed used terms used in physics and mathematics. I will use italic to denote the terminology used in physics. It is safe to ignore them if you just care about the mathematics.

Section 1: Generalities about classical versus quantum systems

In classical physics, we assume that a system’s properties have well-defined values regardless of how we choose to measure them.

Basic terminology

Set of states

The preparation of a system builds a convex set of states as our initial condition for the system.

For a collection of NNN system. Let procedure N1=λP1N_1=\lambda P_1N1​=λP1​ be a preparation procedure for state P1P_1P1​, and N2=(1−λ)P2N_2=(1-\lambda) P_2N2​=(1−λ)P2​ be a preparation procedure for state P2P_2P2​. The state of the collection is N=λN1+(1−λ)N2N=\lambda N_1+(1-\lambda) N_2N=λN1​+(1−λ)N2​.

Set of effects

The set of effects is the set of all possible outcomes of a measurement. Ω={ω1,ω2,…,ωn}\Omega=\{\omega_1, \omega_2, \ldots, \omega_n\}Ω={ω1​,ω2​,…,ωn​}. Where each ωi\omega_iωi​ is an associated effect, or some query problems regarding the system. (For example, is outcome ωi\omega_iωi​ observed?)

Registration of outcomes

A pair of state and effect determines a probability Ei(P)=p(ωi∣P)E_i(P)=p(\omega_i|P)Ei​(P)=p(ωi​∣P). By the law of large numbers, this probability shall converge to N(ωi)/NN(\omega_i)/NN(ωi​)/N as NNN increases.

Quantum states, observables (random variables), and effects can be represented mathematically by linear operators on a Hilbert space.

Section 2: Examples of physical experiment in language of mathematics

Sten-Gernach experiment

State preparation: Silver tams are emitted from a thermal source and collimated to form a beam.

Measurement: Silver atoms interact with the field produced by the magnet and impinges on the class plate.

Registration: The impression left on the glass pace by the condensed silver atoms.

Section 3: Finite probability spaces in the language of Hilbert space and operators

Superposition is a linear combination of two or more states.

A quantum coin can be represented mathematically by linear combination of ∣0⟩|0\rangle∣0⟩ and ∣1⟩|1\rangle∣1⟩.α∣0⟩+β∣1⟩∈H≅C2\alpha|0\rangle+\beta|1\rangle\in\mathscr{H}\cong\mathbb{C}^2α∣0⟩+β∣1⟩∈H≅C2.

For the rest of the material, we shall take the H\mathscr{H}H to be vector space over C\mathbb{C}C.

Definitions in classical probability under generalized probability theory

Definition of states (classical probability)

A state in classical probability is a probability distribution on the set of all possible outcomes. We can list as (p1,p2,⋯ ,pn)(p_1,p_2,\cdots,p_n)(p1​,p2​,⋯,pn​).

To each event A∈ΩA\in \OmegaA∈Ω, we associate the operator on H\mathscr{H}H of multiplication by the indicator function PA≔MIA:f↦IAfP_A\coloneqq M_{\mathbb{I}_A}:f\mapsto \mathbb{I}_A fPA​:=MIA​​:f↦IA​f that projects onto the subspace of H\mathscr{H}H corresponding to the event AAA.

PA=∑k=1nak∣k⟩⟨k∣P_A=\sum_{k=1}^n a_k|k\rangle\langle k|PA​=k=1∑n​ak​∣k⟩⟨k∣

where ak∈{0,1}a_k\in\{0,1\}ak​∈{0,1}, and ak=1a_k=1ak​=1 if and only if k∈Ak\in Ak∈A. Note that PA∗=PAP_A^*=P_APA∗​=PA​ and PA2=PAP_A^2=P_APA2​=PA​.

Definition of density operator (classical probability)

Let (p1,p2,⋯ ,pn)(p_1,p_2,\cdots,p_n)(p1​,p2​,⋯,pn​) be a probability distribution on XXX, where pk≥0p_k\geq 0pk​≥0 and ∑k=1npk=1\sum_{k=1}^n p_k=1∑k=1n​pk​=1. The density operator ρ\rhoρ is defined by

ρ=∑k=1npk∣k⟩⟨k∣\rho=\sum_{k=1}^n p_k|k\rangle\langle k|ρ=k=1∑n​pk​∣k⟩⟨k∣

The probability of event AAA relative to the probability distribution (p1,p2,⋯ ,pn)(p_1,p_2,\cdots,p_n)(p1​,p2​,⋯,pn​) becomes the trace of the product of ρ\rhoρ and PAP_APA​.

Prob⁡ρ(A)≔Tr(ρPA)\operatorname{Prob}_\rho(A)\coloneqq\text{Tr}(\rho P_A)Probρ​(A):=Tr(ρPA​)

Definition of random variables (classical probability)

A random variable is a function f:X→Rf:X\to\mathbb{R}f:X→R. It can also be written in operator form:

F=∑k=1nf(k)P{k}F=\sum_{k=1}^n f(k)P_{\{k\}}F=k=1∑n​f(k)P{k}​

The expectation of fff relative to the probability distribution (p1,p2,⋯ ,pn)(p_1,p_2,\cdots,p_n)(p1​,p2​,⋯,pn​) is given by

Eρ(f)=∑k=1npkf(k)=Tr⁡(ρF)\mathbb{E}_\rho(f)=\sum_{k=1}^n p_k f(k)=\operatorname{Tr}(\rho F)Eρ​(f)=k=1∑n​pk​f(k)=Tr(ρF)

Note, by our definition of the operator F,PA,ρF,P_A,\rhoF,PA​,ρ (all diagonal operators) commute among themselves, which does not hold in general, in non-commutative (quantum) theory.

Section 4: Why we need generalized probability theory to study quantum systems

Story of light polarization and violation of Bell’s inequality.

Classical picture of light polarization and Bell’s inequality

An interesting story will be presented here.

Polarization of light

The light which comes through a polarizer is polarized in a certain direction. If we fixed the first filter and rotate the second filter, we will observe the intensity of the light will change.

The light intensity decreased with α\alphaα (the angle between the two filters). The light should vanished when α=π/2\alpha=\pi/2α=π/2.

By experimental measurement, the intensity of the light passing the first filter is half the beam intensity (Assume the original beam is completely unpolarized).

Then I1=I0/2I_1=I_0/2I1​=I0​/2, and

I2=I0cos⁡2αI_2=I_0\cos^2\alphaI2​=I0​cos2α

Claim: there exist a smallest package of monochromatic light, which is a photon.

We can model the behavior of each individual photon passing through the filter with direction α\alphaα with random variable PαP_\alphaPα​. The Pα(ω)=1P_\alpha(\omega)=1Pα​(ω)=1 if the photon passes through the filter, and Pα(ω)=0P_\alpha(\omega)=0Pα​(ω)=0 if the photon does not pass through the filter.

Then, the probability of the photon passing through the two filters with direction α\alphaα and β\betaβ is given by

E(PαPβ)=Prob⁡(Pα=1 and Pβ=1)=12cos⁡2(α−β)\mathbb{E}(P_\alpha P_\beta)=\operatorname{Prob}(P_\alpha=1 \text{ and } P_\beta=1)=\frac{1}{2}\cos^2(\alpha-\beta)E(Pα​Pβ​)=Prob(Pα​=1 and Pβ​=1)=21​cos2(α−β)

However, for system of 3 polarizing filters F1,F2,F3F_1,F_2,F_3F1​,F2​,F3​, having direction α1,α2,α3\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\alpha_3α1​,α2​,α3​. If we put them on the optical bench in pairs, Then we will have three random variables P1,P2,P3P_1,P_2,P_3P1​,P2​,P3​.

Bell’s 3 variable inequality

Prob⁡(P1=1,P3=0)≤Prob⁡(P1=1,P2=0)+Prob⁡(P2=1,P3=0)\operatorname{Prob}(P_1=1,P_3=0)\leq \operatorname{Prob}(P_1=1,P_2=0)+\operatorname{Prob}(P_2=1,P_3=0)Prob(P1​=1,P3​=0)≤Prob(P1​=1,P2​=0)+Prob(P2​=1,P3​=0)

Proof

By the law of total probability, (The event that the photon passes through the first filter but not the third filter is the union of the event that the photon did not pass through the second filter and the event that the photon passed the second filter and did not pass through the third filter) we have

Prob⁡(P1=1,P3=0)=Prob⁡(P1=1,P2=0,P3=0)+Prob⁡(P1=1,P2=1,P3=0)=Prob⁡(P1=1,P2=0)Prob⁡(P3=0)+Prob⁡(P2=1,P3=0)Prob⁡(P1=1)≤Prob⁡(P1=1,P2=0)+Prob⁡(P2=1,P3=0)\begin{aligned} \operatorname{Prob}(P_1=1,P_3=0)&=\operatorname{Prob}(P_1=1,P_2=0,P_3=0)+\operatorname{Prob}(P_1=1,P_2=1,P_3=0)\\ &=\operatorname{Prob}(P_1=1,P_2=0)\operatorname{Prob}(P_3=0)+\operatorname{Prob}(P_2=1,P_3=0)\operatorname{Prob}(P_1=1)\\ &\leq\operatorname{Prob}(P_1=1,P_2=0)+\operatorname{Prob}(P_2=1,P_3=0) \end{aligned}Prob(P1​=1,P3​=0)​=Prob(P1​=1,P2​=0,P3​=0)+Prob(P1​=1,P2​=1,P3​=0)=Prob(P1​=1,P2​=0)Prob(P3​=0)+Prob(P2​=1,P3​=0)Prob(P1​=1)≤Prob(P1​=1,P2​=0)+Prob(P2​=1,P3​=0)​

However, according to our experimental measurement, for any pair of polarizers Fi,FjF_i,F_jFi​,Fj​, by the complement rule, we have

Prob⁡(Pi=1,Pj=0)=Prob⁡(Pi=1)−Prob⁡(Pi=1,Pj=1)=12−12cos⁡2(αi−αj)=12sin⁡2(αi−αj)\begin{aligned} \operatorname{Prob}(P_i=1,P_j=0)&=\operatorname{Prob}(P_i=1)-\operatorname{Prob}(P_i=1,P_j=1)\\ &=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\cos^2(\alpha_i-\alpha_j)\\ &=\frac{1}{2}\sin^2(\alpha_i-\alpha_j) \end{aligned}Prob(Pi​=1,Pj​=0)​=Prob(Pi​=1)−Prob(Pi​=1,Pj​=1)=21​−21​cos2(αi​−αj​)=21​sin2(αi​−αj​)​

This leads to a contradiction if we apply the inequality to the experimental data.

12sin⁡2(α1−α3)≤12sin⁡2(α1−α2)+12sin⁡2(α2−α3)\frac{1}{2}\sin^2(\alpha_1-\alpha_3)\leq\frac{1}{2}\sin^2(\alpha_1-\alpha_2)+\frac{1}{2}\sin^2(\alpha_2-\alpha_3)21​sin2(α1​−α3​)≤21​sin2(α1​−α2​)+21​sin2(α2​−α3​)

If α1=0,α2=π6,α3=π3\alpha_1=0,\alpha_2=\frac{\pi}{6},\alpha_3=\frac{\pi}{3}α1​=0,α2​=6π​,α3​=3π​, then

12sin⁡2(−π3)≤12sin⁡2(−π6)+12sin⁡2(π6−π3)38≤18+1838≤14\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2}\sin^2(-\frac{\pi}{3})&\leq\frac{1}{2}\sin^2(-\frac{\pi}{6})+\frac{1}{2}\sin^2(\frac{\pi}{6}-\frac{\pi}{3})\\ \frac{3}{8}&\leq\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{8}\\ \frac{3}{8}&\leq\frac{1}{4} \end{aligned}21​sin2(−3π​)83​83​​≤21​sin2(−6π​)+21​sin2(6π​−3π​)≤81​+81​≤41​​

This is a contradiction, so Bell’s inequality is violated.

Other revised experiments (eg. Aspect’s experiment, Calcium entangled photon experiment) are also conducted and the inequality is still violated.

The true model of light polarization

The full description of the light polarization is given belows:

State of polarization of a photon: ψ=α∣0⟩+β∣1⟩\psi=\alpha|0\rangle+\beta|1\rangleψ=α∣0⟩+β∣1⟩, where ∣0⟩|0\rangle∣0⟩ and ∣1⟩|1\rangle∣1⟩ are the two orthogonal polarization states in C2\mathbb{C}^2C2.

Polarization filter (generalized 0,1 valued random variable): orthogonal projection PαP_\alphaPα​ on C2\mathbb{C}^2C2 corresponding to the direction α\alphaα. (operator satisfies Pα∗=Pα=Pα2P_\alpha^*=P_\alpha=P_\alpha^2Pα∗​=Pα​=Pα2​.)

The matrix representation of PαP_\alphaPα​ is given by

Pα=(cos⁡2(α)cos⁡(α)sin⁡(α)cos⁡(α)sin⁡(α)sin⁡2(α))P_\alpha=\begin{pmatrix} \cos^2(\alpha) & \cos(\alpha)\sin(\alpha)\\ \cos(\alpha)\sin(\alpha) & \sin^2(\alpha) \end{pmatrix}Pα​=(cos2(α)cos(α)sin(α)​cos(α)sin(α)sin2(α)​)

Probability of a photon passing through the filter PαP_\alphaPα​ is given by ⟨Pαψ,ψ⟩\langle P_\alpha\psi,\psi\rangle⟨Pα​ψ,ψ⟩, this is cos⁡2(α)\cos^2(\alpha)cos2(α) if we set ψ=∣0⟩\psi=|0\rangleψ=∣0⟩.

Since the probability of a photon passing through the three filters is not commutative, it is impossible to discuss Prob⁡(P1=1,P3=0)\operatorname{Prob}(P_1=1,P_3=0)Prob(P1​=1,P3​=0) in the classical setting.

Section 5: The non-commutative (quantum) probability theory

Let H\mathscr{H}H be a Hilbert space. H\mathscr{H}H consists of complex-valued functions on a finite set Ω={1,2,⋯ ,n}\Omega=\{1,2,\cdots,n\}Ω={1,2,⋯,n}. and that the functions (e1,e2,⋯ ,en)(e_1,e_2,\cdots,e_n)(e1​,e2​,⋯,en​) form an orthonormal basis of H\mathscr{H}H. We use Dirac notation ∣k⟩|k\rangle∣k⟩ to denote the basis vector eke_kek​.

In classical settings, multiplication operators is now be the full space of bounded linear operators on H\mathscr{H}H. (Denoted by B(H)\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{H})B(H))

Let A,B∈FA,B\in\mathscr{F}A,B∈F be the set of all events in the classical probability settings. XXX denotes the set of all possible outcomes.

A orthogonal projection on a Hilbert space is a projection operator satisfying P∗=PP^*=PP∗=P and P2=PP^2=PP2=P. We denote the set of all orthogonal projections on H\mathscr{H}H by P\mathscr{P}P.

This can be found in linear algebra. Orthogonal projection 

Let P,Q∈PP,Q\in\mathscr{P}P,Q∈P be the event in non-commutative (quantum) probability space. R(⋅)R(\cdot)R(⋅) is the range of the operator. P⊥P^\perpP⊥ is the orthogonal complement of PPP.

ClassicalClassical interpretationNon-commutative (Quantum)Non-commutative (Quantum) interpretation
A⊂BA\subset BA⊂BEvent AAA is a subset of event BBBP≤QP\leq QP≤QR(P)⊆R(Q)R(P)\subseteq R(Q)R(P)⊆R(Q) Range of event PPP is a subset of range of event QQQ
A∩BA\cap BA∩BBoth event AAA and BBB happenedP∧QP\land QP∧Qprojection to R(P)∩R(Q)R(P)\cap R(Q)R(P)∩R(Q) Range of event PPP and event QQQ happened
A∪BA\cup BA∪BAny of the event AAA or BBB happenedP∨QP\lor QP∨Qprojection to R(P)∪R(Q)R(P)\cup R(Q)R(P)∪R(Q) Range of event PPP or event QQQ happened
X⊂AX\subset AX⊂A or AcA^cAcEvent AAA did not happenP⊥P^\perpP⊥projectionR(P)⊥R(P)^\perpR(P)⊥ Range of event PPP is the orthogonal complement of range of event PPP

In such setting, some rules of classical probability theory are not valid in quantum probability theory.

In classical probability theory, A∩(B∪C)=(A∩B)∪(A∩C)A\cap(B\cup C)=(A\cap B)\cup(A\cap C)A∩(B∪C)=(A∩B)∪(A∩C).

In quantum probability theory, P∧(Q∨R)≠(P∧Q)∨(P∧R)P\land(Q\lor R)\neq(P\land Q)\lor(P\land R)P∧(Q∨R)=(P∧Q)∨(P∧R) in general.

Definitions of non-commutative (quantum) probability theory under generalized probability theory

Definition of states (non-commutative (quantum) probability theory)

A state on (B(H),P)(\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{H}),\mathscr{P})(B(H),P) is a map μ:P→[0,1]\mu:\mathscr{P}\to[0,1]μ:P→[0,1] such that:

  1. μ(O)=0\mu(O)=0μ(O)=0, where OOO is the zero projection.
  2. If P1,P2,⋯ ,PnP_1,P_2,\cdots,P_nP1​,P2​,⋯,Pn​ are pairwise disjoint orthogonal projections, then μ(P1∨P2∨⋯∨Pn)=∑i=1nμ(Pi)\mu(P_1\lor P_2\lor\cdots\lor P_n)=\sum_{i=1}^n\mu(P_i)μ(P1​∨P2​∨⋯∨Pn​)=∑i=1n​μ(Pi​).

Where projections are disjoint if PiPj=PjPi=OP_iP_j=P_jP_i=OPi​Pj​=Pj​Pi​=O.

Definition of density operator (non-commutative (quantum) probability theory)

A density operator ρ\rhoρ on the finite-dimensional Hilbert space H\mathscr{H}H is:

  1. self-adjoint (A∗=AA^*=AA∗=A, that is ⟨Ax,y⟩=⟨x,Ay⟩\langle Ax,y\rangle=\langle x,Ay\rangle⟨Ax,y⟩=⟨x,Ay⟩ for all x,y∈Hx,y\in\mathscr{H}x,y∈H)
  2. positive semi-definite (all eigenvalues are non-negative)
  3. Tr⁡(ρ)=1\operatorname{Tr}(\rho)=1Tr(ρ)=1.

If (∣ψ1⟩,∣ψ2⟩,⋯ ,∣ψn⟩)(|\psi_1\rangle,|\psi_2\rangle,\cdots,|\psi_n\rangle)(∣ψ1​⟩,∣ψ2​⟩,⋯,∣ψn​⟩) is an orthonormal basis of H\mathscr{H}H consisting of eigenvectors of ρ\rhoρ, for the eigenvalue p1,p2,⋯ ,pnp_1,p_2,\cdots,p_np1​,p2​,⋯,pn​, then pj≥0p_j\geq 0pj​≥0 and ∑j=1npj=1\sum_{j=1}^n p_j=1∑j=1n​pj​=1.

We can write ρ\rhoρ as

ρ=∑j=1npj∣ψj⟩⟨ψj∣\rho=\sum_{j=1}^n p_j|\psi_j\rangle\langle\psi_j|ρ=j=1∑n​pj​∣ψj​⟩⟨ψj​∣

(under basis ∣ψj⟩|\psi_j\rangle∣ψj​⟩, it is a diagonal matrix with pjp_jpj​ on the diagonal)

Every basis of H\mathscr{H}H can be decomposed to these forms.

Theorem: Born’s rule

Let ρ\rhoρ be a density operator on H\mathscr{H}H. then

μ(P)≔Tr⁡(ρP)=∑j=1npj⟨ψj∣P∣ψj⟩\mu(P)\coloneqq\operatorname{Tr}(\rho P)=\sum_{j=1}^n p_j\langle\psi_j|P|\psi_j\rangleμ(P):=Tr(ρP)=j=1∑n​pj​⟨ψj​∣P∣ψj​⟩

Defines a probability measure on the space P\mathscr{P}P.

[Proof ignored here]

Theorem: Gleason’s theorem (very important)

Let H\mathscr{H}H be a Hilbert space over C\mathbb{C}C or R\mathbb{R}R of dimension n≥3n\geq 3n≥3. Let μ\muμ be a state on the space P\mathscr{P}P of projections on H\mathscr{H}H. Then there exists a unique density operator ρ\rhoρ such that

μ(P)=Tr⁡(ρP)\mu(P)=\operatorname{Tr}(\rho P)μ(P)=Tr(ρP)

for all P∈PP\in\mathscr{P}P∈P. P\mathscr{P}P is the space of all orthogonal projections on H\mathscr{H}H.

[Proof ignored here]

Definition of random variable or Observables (non-commutative (quantum) probability theory)

It is the physical measurement of a system that we are interested in. (kinetic energy, position, momentum, etc.)

B(R)\mathscr{B}(\mathbb{R})B(R) is the set of all Borel sets on R\mathbb{R}R.

An random variable on the Hilbert space H\mathscr{H}H is a projection valued map P:B(R)→PP:\mathscr{B}(\mathbb{R})\to\mathscr{P}P:B(R)→P.

With the following properties:

  1. P(∅)=OP(\emptyset)=OP(∅)=O (the zero projection)
  2. P(R)=IP(\mathbb{R})=IP(R)=I (the identity projection)
  3. For any sequence A1,A2,⋯ ,An∈B(R)A_1,A_2,\cdots,A_n\in \mathscr{B}(\mathbb{R})A1​,A2​,⋯,An​∈B(R). the following holds:
    (a) P(⋃i=1nAi)=⋁i=1nP(Ai)P(\bigcup_{i=1}^n A_i)=\bigvee_{i=1}^n P(A_i)P(⋃i=1n​Ai​)=⋁i=1n​P(Ai​)
    (b) P(⋂i=1nAi)=⋀i=1nP(Ai)P(\bigcap_{i=1}^n A_i)=\bigwedge_{i=1}^n P(A_i)P(⋂i=1n​Ai​)=⋀i=1n​P(Ai​)
    (c) P(Ac)=I−P(A)P(A^c)=I-P(A)P(Ac)=I−P(A) (d) If AjA_jAj​ are mutually disjoint (that is P(Ai)P(Aj)=P(Aj)P(Ai)=OP(A_i)P(A_j)=P(A_j)P(A_i)=OP(Ai​)P(Aj​)=P(Aj​)P(Ai​)=O for i≠ji\neq ji=j), then P(⋃j=1nAj)=∑j=1nP(Aj)P(\bigcup_{j=1}^n A_j)=\sum_{j=1}^n P(A_j)P(⋃j=1n​Aj​)=∑j=1n​P(Aj​)

Definition of probability of a random variable

For a system prepared in state ρ\rhoρ, the probability of the random variable by the projection-valued measure PPP is in the Borel set AAA is Tr⁡(ρP(A))\operatorname{Tr}(\rho P(A))Tr(ρP(A)).

Expectation of an random variable and projective measurement

Notice that if we set λ\lambdaλ is observed with probability pλ=Tr⁡(ρPλ)p_\lambda=\operatorname{Tr}(\rho P_\lambda)pλ​=Tr(ρPλ​). ρ′≔∑λ∈sp(T)PλρPλ\rho'\coloneqq\sum_{\lambda\in sp(T)}P_\lambda \rho P_\lambdaρ′:=∑λ∈sp(T)​Pλ​ρPλ​ is a density operator.

Definition of expectation of operators

Let TTT be a self-adjoint operator on H\mathscr{H}H. The expectation of TTT relative to the density operator ρ\rhoρ is given by

Eρ(T)=Tr⁡(ρT)=∑λ∈sp(T)λTr⁡(ρP(λ))\mathbb{E}_\rho(T)=\operatorname{Tr}(\rho T)=\sum_{\lambda\in sp(T)}\lambda \operatorname{Tr}(\rho P(\lambda))Eρ​(T)=Tr(ρT)=λ∈sp(T)∑​λTr(ρP(λ))

if we set T=∑λ∈sp(T)λPλT=\sum_{\lambda\in sp(T)}\lambda P_\lambdaT=∑λ∈sp(T)​λPλ​, then Eρ(T)=∑λ∈sp(T)λTr⁡(ρP(λ))\mathbb{E}_\rho(T)=\sum_{\lambda\in sp(T)}\lambda \operatorname{Tr}(\rho P(\lambda))Eρ​(T)=∑λ∈sp(T)​λTr(ρP(λ)).

The uncertainty principle

Let A,BA,BA,B be two self-adjoint operators on H\mathscr{H}H. Then we define the following two self-adjoint operators:

i[A,B]≔i(AB−BA)i[A,B]\coloneqq i(AB-BA)i[A,B]:=i(AB−BA) A∘B≔AB+BA2A\circ B\coloneqq \frac{AB+BA}{2}A∘B:=2AB+BA​

Note that A∘BA\circ BA∘B satisfies Jordan’s identity.

(A∘B)∘(A∘A)=A∘(B∘(A∘A))(A\circ B)\circ (A\circ A)=A\circ (B\circ (A\circ A))(A∘B)∘(A∘A)=A∘(B∘(A∘A))

Definition of variance

Given a state ρ\rhoρ, the variance of AAA is given by

Var⁡ρ(A)≔Eρ(A2)−Eρ(A)2=Tr⁡(ρA2)−Tr⁡(ρA)2\operatorname{Var}_\rho(A)\coloneqq\mathbb{E}_\rho(A^2)-\mathbb{E}_\rho(A)^2=\operatorname{Tr}(\rho A^2)-\operatorname{Tr}(\rho A)^2Varρ​(A):=Eρ​(A2)−Eρ​(A)2=Tr(ρA2)−Tr(ρA)2

Definition of covariance

Given a state ρ\rhoρ, the covariance of AAA and BBB is given by the Jordan product of AAA and BBB.

Cov⁡ρ(A,B)≔Eρ(A∘B)−Eρ(A)Eρ(B)=Tr⁡(ρA∘B)−Tr⁡(ρA)Tr⁡(ρB)\operatorname{Cov}_\rho(A,B)\coloneqq\mathbb{E}_\rho(A\circ B)-\mathbb{E}_\rho(A)\mathbb{E}_\rho(B)=\operatorname{Tr}(\rho A\circ B)-\operatorname{Tr}(\rho A)\operatorname{Tr}(\rho B)Covρ​(A,B):=Eρ​(A∘B)−Eρ​(A)Eρ​(B)=Tr(ρA∘B)−Tr(ρA)Tr(ρB)

Robertson-Schrödinger uncertainty relation in finite dimensional Hilbert space

Let ρ\rhoρ be a state on H\mathscr{H}H, A,BA,BA,B be two self-adjoint operators on H\mathscr{H}H. Then

Var⁡ρ(A)Var⁡ρ(B)≥Cov⁡ρ(A,B)2+14∣Eρ([A,B])∣2\operatorname{Var}_\rho(A)\operatorname{Var}_\rho(B)\geq\operatorname{Cov}_\rho(A,B)^2+\frac{1}{4}|\mathbb{E}_\rho([A,B])|^2Varρ​(A)Varρ​(B)≥Covρ​(A,B)2+41​∣Eρ​([A,B])∣2

If ρ\rhoρ is a pure state (ρ=∣ψ⟩⟨ψ∣\rho=|\psi\rangle\langle\psi|ρ=∣ψ⟩⟨ψ∣ for some unit vector ∣ψ⟩∈H|\psi\rangle\in\mathscr{H}∣ψ⟩∈H), and the equality holds, then AAA and BBB are collinear (i.e. A=cBA=c BA=cB for some constant c∈Rc\in\mathbb{R}c∈R).

When AAA and BBB commute, the classical inequality is recovered (Cauchy-Schwarz inequality).

Var⁡ρ(A)Var⁡ρ(B)≥Cov⁡ρ(A,B)2\operatorname{Var}_\rho(A)\operatorname{Var}_\rho(B)\geq\operatorname{Cov}_\rho(A,B)^2Varρ​(A)Varρ​(B)≥Covρ​(A,B)2

[Proof ignored here]

The uncertainty relation for unbounded symmetric operators

Definition of symmetric operator

An operator AAA is symmetric if for all ϕ,ψ∈H\phi,\psi\in\mathscr{H}ϕ,ψ∈H, we have

⟨Aϕ,ψ⟩=⟨ϕ,Aψ⟩\langle A\phi,\psi\rangle=\langle\phi,A\psi\rangle⟨Aϕ,ψ⟩=⟨ϕ,Aψ⟩

An example of symmetric operator is T(ψ)=iℏdψdxT(\psi)=i\hbar\frac{d\psi}{dx}T(ψ)=iℏdxdψ​. If we let H=D(T)\mathscr{H}=\mathscr{D}(T)H=D(T), ℏ\hbarℏ is the Planck constant.

D(T)\mathscr{D}(T)D(T) be the space of all square integrable, differentiable, and it’s derivative is also square integrable functions on R\mathbb{R}R.

Definition of joint domain of two operators

Let (A,D(A)),(B,D(B))(A,\mathscr{D}(A)),(B,\mathscr{D}(B))(A,D(A)),(B,D(B)) be two symmetric operators on their corresponding domains. The domain of ABABAB is defined as

D(AB)≔{ψ∈D(B):Bψ∈D(A)}\mathscr{D}(AB)\coloneqq\{\psi\in\mathscr{D}(B):B\psi\in\mathscr{D}(A)\}D(AB):={ψ∈D(B):Bψ∈D(A)}

Since (AB)ψ=A(Bψ)(AB)\psi=A(B\psi)(AB)ψ=A(Bψ), the variance of an operator AAA relative to a pure state ρ=∣ψ⟩⟨ψ∣\rho=|\psi\rangle\langle\psi|ρ=∣ψ⟩⟨ψ∣ is given by

Var⁡ρ(A)=Tr⁡(ρA2)−Tr⁡(ρA)2=⟨ψ,A2ψ⟩−⟨ψ,Aψ⟩2\operatorname{Var}_\rho(A)=\operatorname{Tr}(\rho A^2)-\operatorname{Tr}(\rho A)^2=\langle\psi,A^2\psi\rangle-\langle\psi,A\psi\rangle^2Varρ​(A)=Tr(ρA2)−Tr(ρA)2=⟨ψ,A2ψ⟩−⟨ψ,Aψ⟩2

If AAA is symmetric, then Var⁡ρ(A)=⟨Aψ,Aψ⟩−⟨ψ,Aψ⟩2\operatorname{Var}_\rho(A)=\langle A\psi,A\psi\rangle-\langle \psi, A\psi\rangle^2Varρ​(A)=⟨Aψ,Aψ⟩−⟨ψ,Aψ⟩2.

Robertson-Schrödinger uncertainty relation for unbounded symmetric operators

Let (A,D(A)),(B,D(B))(A,\mathscr{D}(A)),(B,\mathscr{D}(B))(A,D(A)),(B,D(B)) be two symmetric operators on their corresponding domains. Then

Var⁡ρ(A)Var⁡ρ(B)≥Cov⁡ρ(A,B)2+14∣Eρ([A,B])∣2\operatorname{Var}_\rho(A)\operatorname{Var}_\rho(B)\geq\operatorname{Cov}_\rho(A,B)^2+\frac{1}{4}|\mathbb{E}_\rho([A,B])|^2Varρ​(A)Varρ​(B)≥Covρ​(A,B)2+41​∣Eρ​([A,B])∣2

If ρ\rhoρ is a pure state (ρ=∣ψ⟩⟨ψ∣\rho=|\psi\rangle\langle\psi|ρ=∣ψ⟩⟨ψ∣ for some unit vector ∣ψ⟩∈H|\psi\rangle\in\mathscr{H}∣ψ⟩∈H), and the equality holds, then AψA\psiAψ and BψB\psiBψ are collinear (i.e. Aψ=cBψA\psi=c B\psiAψ=cBψ for some constant c∈Rc\in\mathbb{R}c∈R).

[Proof ignored here]

Summary of analog of classical probability theory and non-commutative (quantum) probability theory

Classical probabilityNon-commutative (Quantum) probability
Sample space Ω\OmegaΩ, cardinality ∣Ω∣=n\vert\Omega\vert=n∣Ω∣=n, example: Ω={0,1}\Omega=\{0,1\}Ω={0,1}Complex Hilbert space H\mathscr{H}H, dimension dim⁡H=n\dim\mathscr{H}=ndimH=n, example: H=C2\mathscr{H}=\mathbb{C}^2H=C2
Common algebra of C\mathbb{C}C valued functionsAlgebra of bounded operators B(H)\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{H})B(H)
f↦fˉf\mapsto \bar{f}f↦fˉ​ complex conjugationP↦P∗P\mapsto P^*P↦P∗ adjoint
Events: indicator functions of setsProjections: space of orthogonal projections P⊆B(H)\mathscr{P}\subseteq\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{H})P⊆B(H)
functions fff such that f2=f=f‾f^2=f=\overline{f}f2=f=f​orthogonal projections PPP such that P∗=P=P2P^*=P=P^2P∗=P=P2
R\mathbb{R}R-valued functions f=f‾f=\overline{f}f=f​self-adjoint operators A=A∗A=A^*A=A∗
If−1({λ})\mathbb{I}_{f^{-1}(\{\lambda\})}If−1({λ})​ is the indicator function of the set f−1({λ})f^{-1}(\{\lambda\})f−1({λ})P(λ)P(\lambda)P(λ) is the orthogonal projection to eigenspace
f=∑λ∈Range⁡(f)λIf−1({λ})f=\sum_{\lambda\in \operatorname{Range}(f)}\lambda \mathbb{I}_{f^{-1}(\{\lambda\})}f=∑λ∈Range(f)​λIf−1({λ})​A=∑λ∈sp⁡(A)λP(λ)A=\sum_{\lambda\in \operatorname{sp}(A)}\lambda P(\lambda)A=∑λ∈sp(A)​λP(λ)
Probability measure μ\muμ on Ω\OmegaΩDensity operator ρ\rhoρ on H\mathscr{H}H
Delta measure δω\delta_\omegaδω​Pure state ρ=∣ψ⟩⟨ψ∣\rho=\vert\psi\rangle\langle\psi\vertρ=∣ψ⟩⟨ψ∣
μ\muμ is non-negative measure and ∑i=1nμ({i})=1\sum_{i=1}^n\mu(\{i\})=1∑i=1n​μ({i})=1ρ\rhoρ is positive semi-definite and Tr⁡(ρ)=1\operatorname{Tr}(\rho)=1Tr(ρ)=1
Expected value of random variable fff is Eμ(f)=∑i=1nf(i)μ({i})\mathbb{E}_{\mu}(f)=\sum_{i=1}^n f(i)\mu(\{i\})Eμ​(f)=∑i=1n​f(i)μ({i})Expected value of operator AAA is Eρ(A)=Tr⁡(ρA)\mathbb{E}_\rho(A)=\operatorname{Tr}(\rho A)Eρ​(A)=Tr(ρA)
Variance of random variable fff is Var⁡μ(f)=∑i=1n(f(i)−Eμ(f))2μ({i})\operatorname{Var}_\mu(f)=\sum_{i=1}^n (f(i)-\mathbb{E}_\mu(f))^2\mu(\{i\})Varμ​(f)=∑i=1n​(f(i)−Eμ​(f))2μ({i})Variance of operator AAA is Var⁡ρ(A)=Tr⁡(ρA2)−Tr⁡(ρA)2\operatorname{Var}_\rho(A)=\operatorname{Tr}(\rho A^2)-\operatorname{Tr}(\rho A)^2Varρ​(A)=Tr(ρA2)−Tr(ρA)2
Covariance of random variables fff and ggg is Cov⁡μ(f,g)=∑i=1n(f(i)−Eμ(f))(g(i)−Eμ(g))μ({i})\operatorname{Cov}_\mu(f,g)=\sum_{i=1}^n (f(i)-\mathbb{E}_\mu(f))(g(i)-\mathbb{E}_\mu(g))\mu(\{i\})Covμ​(f,g)=∑i=1n​(f(i)−Eμ​(f))(g(i)−Eμ​(g))μ({i})Covariance of operators AAA and BBB is Cov⁡ρ(A,B)=Tr⁡(ρA∘B)−Tr⁡(ρA)Tr⁡(ρB)\operatorname{Cov}_\rho(A,B)=\operatorname{Tr}(\rho A\circ B)-\operatorname{Tr}(\rho A)\operatorname{Tr}(\rho B)Covρ​(A,B)=Tr(ρA∘B)−Tr(ρA)Tr(ρB)
Composite system is given by Cartesian product of the sample spaces Ω1×Ω2\Omega_1\times\Omega_2Ω1​×Ω2​Composite system is given by tensor product of the Hilbert spaces H1⊗H2\mathscr{H}_1\otimes\mathscr{H}_2H1​⊗H2​
Product measure μ1×μ2\mu_1\times\mu_2μ1​×μ2​ on Ω1×Ω2\Omega_1\times\Omega_2Ω1​×Ω2​Tensor product of space ρ1⊗ρ2\rho_1\otimes\rho_2ρ1​⊗ρ2​ on H1⊗H2\mathscr{H}_1\otimes\mathscr{H}_2H1​⊗H2​
Marginal distribution π∗v\pi_*vπ∗​vPartial trace Tr⁡2(ρ)\operatorname{Tr}_2(\rho)Tr2​(ρ)

States of two dimensional system and the complex projective space (Bloch sphere)

Let v=eiθuv=e^{i\theta}uv=eiθu, then the space of pure states (ρ=∣u⟩⟨u∣\rho=|u\rangle\langle u|ρ=∣u⟩⟨u∣) is the complex projective space CP1\mathbb{C}P^1CP1.

α=xi+iyi,β=x2+iy2\alpha=x_i+iy_i,\beta=x_2+iy_2α=xi​+iyi​,β=x2​+iy2​ must satisfy ∣α∣2+∣β∣2=1|\alpha|^2+|\beta|^2=1∣α∣2+∣β∣2=1, that is x12+x22+y12+y22=1x_1^2+x_2^2+y_1^2+y_2^2=1x12​+x22​+y12​+y22​=1.

The set of unit vectors in C2\mathbb{C}^2C2 is the unit sphere in R3\mathbb{R}^3R3.

So the space of pure states is the unit circle in R2\mathbb{R}^2R2.

Mapping between the space of pure states and the complex projective space

Any two dimensional pure state can be written as eiθue^{i\theta}ueiθu, where uuu is a unit vector in R2\mathbb{R}^2R2. There exists a bijective map P:S2→P1⊆M2(C)P:S^2\to\mathscr{P}_1\subseteq M_2(\mathbb{C})P:S2→P1​⊆M2​(C) such that P(u)=∣u⟩⟨u∣P(u)=|u\rangle\langle u|P(u)=∣u⟩⟨u∣.

P(x⃗)=12(I+a⃗⋅σ⃗)=12(1001)+ax2(0110)+ay2(0−ii0)+az2(100−1)P(\vec{x})=\frac{1}{2}(I+\vec{a}\cdot\vec{\sigma})=\frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1&0\\ 0&1 \end{pmatrix}+\frac{a_x}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 0&1\\ 1&0 \end{pmatrix}+\frac{a_y}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 0&-i\\ i&0 \end{pmatrix}+\frac{a_z}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1&0\\ 0&-1 \end{pmatrix}P(x)=21​(I+a⋅σ)=21​(10​01​)+2ax​​(01​10​)+2ay​​(0i​−i0​)+2az​​(10​0−1​)
